questions.prompt: PASSAGE A: "The public library is the last building in town that asks nothing of you. There is no entrance fee, no membership tier, no algorithm tracking what you linger over. You walk in, and the building presumes you belong. In an age that monetizes attention by the second, this presumption is not a quaint inheritance from a slower era; it is a quiet act of resistance." — Which of the following best states the writer's central claim?
choices: Public libraries should expand their digital offerings to compete with technology companies.
The unconditional access offered by public libraries challenges the surveillance and gatekeeping that characterize modern commercial spaces.
Libraries are outdated institutions surviving only because they are sustained by nostalgia.
Most public spaces have become hostile to people who lack disposable income.
questions.correctText: The unconditional access offered by public libraries challenges the surveillance and gatekeeping that characterize modern commercial spaces.
questions.explanation: The passage contrasts the library's open access with an age that 'monetizes attention by the second' and frames its presumption of belonging as 'a quiet act of resistance.' That is the central claim. Option C contradicts the passage's admiring tone; A and D are unsupported.

questions.prompt: PASSAGE A: The phrase "a quiet act of resistance" most clearly serves which rhetorical purpose?
choices: It concedes a counterargument by acknowledging that libraries are politically controversial.
It elevates an ordinary civic institution by attaching to it the moral weight of dissent.
It signals that the writer intends to recommend specific legislative reforms.
It introduces a shift from logical argument to anecdotal evidence.
questions.correctText: It elevates an ordinary civic institution by attaching to it the moral weight of dissent.
questions.explanation: By calling library access a form of 'resistance,' the writer recasts a mundane civic service in moral and political terms — a common rhetorical move that asks readers to see a familiar thing as more consequential than they had assumed.

questions.prompt: PASSAGE A: The semicolon in the final sentence ("...slower era; it is a quiet act of resistance") serves primarily to —
choices: introduce a list of supporting examples that follow.
signal a tonal shift from analytical to sentimental.
balance two contrasting characterizations of the same idea, sharpening the reversal.
indicate an interruption or aside that the reader may skip.
questions.correctText: balance two contrasting characterizations of the same idea, sharpening the reversal.
questions.explanation: The semicolon joins two independent clauses that present the same subject (the library's presumption of belonging) under two opposed framings — quaint inheritance vs. quiet resistance. The construction sharpens the writer's pivot from a dismissive to an admiring view.

questions.prompt: PASSAGE A: The writer's reference to algorithms that track "what you linger over" functions chiefly as —
choices: an appeal to authority that cites academic research on surveillance.
an implied contrast that defines the library by what it refuses to do.
a hypothetical scenario the writer concedes is exaggerated.
an anecdote drawn from the writer's personal experience.
questions.correctText: an implied contrast that defines the library by what it refuses to do.
questions.explanation: The image of tracking algorithms is set against the library's open door; the comparison defines the library negatively (by what it does not do) rather than through any direct description. There is no citation, no concession, and no personal narrative.

questions.prompt: PASSAGE A: The passage is most clearly addressed to —
choices: policymakers considering whether to defund municipal libraries.
library staff seeking guidance on serving difficult patrons.
general readers who may take the library's openness for granted and have not considered its civic meaning.
specialists in information science familiar with debates over patron privacy.
questions.correctText: general readers who may take the library's openness for granted and have not considered its civic meaning.
questions.explanation: The op-ed tone, the absence of technical jargon, and the framing 'in an age that monetizes attention' all point to a general readership the writer hopes to defamiliarize — making them see something ordinary in a new light. There is no policy ask or industry-insider vocabulary.

questions.prompt: PASSAGE A: Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the writer's argument?
choices: Several large municipal library systems have begun installing facial-recognition cameras and selling anonymized circulation data to advertisers.
Library attendance has declined in many cities over the past decade as patrons rely more on the internet.
Libraries depend on public funding that fluctuates with municipal budgets.
Some libraries charge nominal fines for overdue materials.
questions.correctText: Several large municipal library systems have begun installing facial-recognition cameras and selling anonymized circulation data to advertisers.
questions.explanation: The writer's specific claim is that libraries do NOT track patrons the way commercial spaces do; evidence that libraries actually do surveil patrons would directly undercut that claim. The other options address tangential issues (attendance, funding, fines) but leave the core surveillance contrast intact.

questions.prompt: PASSAGE B: "Four score and seven years ago our fathers brought forth on this continent, a new nation, conceived in Liberty, and dedicated to the proposition that all men are created equal." (Abraham Lincoln, Gettysburg Address, 1863) — The opening clause "Four score and seven years ago" most likely serves to —
choices: introduce a scientific calculation that grounds the speech in measurable fact.
evoke biblical cadence in order to lend the founding of the nation a sacred, generational weight.
challenge the historical accuracy of conventional accounts of the Revolution.
delay the central point in order to test the patience of a hostile audience.
questions.correctText: evoke biblical cadence in order to lend the founding of the nation a sacred, generational weight.
questions.explanation: The archaic 'four score and seven' (rather than 'eighty-seven') echoes the King James Bible's measurement of human lifespans. Lincoln borrows that cadence to mark the nation's founding as a sacred event handed down across generations.

questions.prompt: PASSAGE B: "...we cannot dedicate — we cannot consecrate — we cannot hallow — this ground." (Lincoln, Gettysburg Address) — The repeated parallel structure most directly creates which effect?
choices: It enumerates a list of distinct ceremonies the speaker plans to conduct.
It produces a cumulative emphasis on the speaker's humility before what the soldiers have already accomplished.
It introduces three competing definitions of the word 'dedicate' for the audience to consider.
It signals that the speaker will retract these statements in the following sentence.
questions.correctText: It produces a cumulative emphasis on the speaker's humility before what the soldiers have already accomplished.
questions.explanation: The triadic repetition of 'we cannot' — paired with the rising intensity of 'dedicate / consecrate / hallow' — drives home the speaker's insistence that the ground has already been hallowed by the soldiers' sacrifice; the parallelism amplifies humility, not enumeration.

questions.prompt: PASSAGE B: "It is rather for us to be here dedicated to the great task remaining before us...that government of the people, by the people, for the people, shall not perish from the earth." (Lincoln, Gettysburg Address) — The speaker's primary purpose in this closing is to —
choices: memorialize individual soldiers by name in order to comfort their families.
redirect the audience from mourning the dead to recommitting themselves to an unfinished cause.
propose a new constitutional amendment limiting the powers of the executive.
concede that the Union's cause has been irreparably damaged by the battle.
questions.correctText: redirect the audience from mourning the dead to recommitting themselves to an unfinished cause.
questions.explanation: The closing pivots from 'these honored dead' to 'the great task remaining' — a classic exhortative move that converts grief into renewed commitment. Naming, legislation, and concession are all absent.

questions.prompt: PASSAGE B: The famous tricolon "of the people, by the people, for the people" achieves its persuasive power primarily through —
choices: a periodic sentence structure that withholds the subject until the end.
an extended metaphor comparing government to a living organism.
the repetition of a single noun across shifting prepositions, which fuses the people with every dimension of governance.
the deliberate violation of grammatical parallelism in order to surprise the listener.
questions.correctText: the repetition of a single noun across shifting prepositions, which fuses the people with every dimension of governance.
questions.explanation: Each prepositional phrase keeps the same noun ('the people') and varies only the relationship — origin, agency, beneficiary. The construction inseparably binds the citizenry to government's source, mechanism, and end.

questions.prompt: PASSAGE B: Lincoln's appeal in the Gettysburg Address relies most heavily on which classical mode?
choices: Logos — building a chain of statistical evidence about Union casualties.
Pathos in service of ethos — invoking shared sacrifice to establish moral standing for the call to action.
Pure ethos — citing the speaker's personal credentials as a wartime commander.
Kairos — naming specific legislation pending before Congress.
questions.correctText: Pathos in service of ethos — invoking shared sacrifice to establish moral standing for the call to action.
questions.explanation: Lincoln offers no statistics or biography. He mobilizes the audience's emotional debt to the dead (pathos) precisely to earn the moral authority (ethos) needed for his closing exhortation. The two appeals work together.

questions.prompt: PASSAGE B: Which feature of the address most strongly suggests that Lincoln intends his words to outlast the immediate occasion of the cemetery dedication?
choices: The reliance on first-person plural pronouns throughout the speech.
The repeated invocation of a founding 'proposition' and a 'new birth of freedom' that frame the war as a universal test of self-government.
The careful avoidance of any reference to the Civil War.
The decision to address the audience only as 'fellow citizens.'
questions.correctText: The repeated invocation of a founding 'proposition' and a 'new birth of freedom' that frame the war as a universal test of self-government.
questions.explanation: By framing Gettysburg as a test of whether any nation 'so conceived' can endure, Lincoln lifts the local dedication into a universal question. That abstraction — not the pronouns or the audience address — is what gives the speech transhistorical reach.

questions.prompt: PASSAGE C: "Every notification is a small, polite demand. Considered one at a time, none is unreasonable. Considered together, they describe a working day that belongs less and less to the worker. We have been told that the device makes us more productive. We have not been asked whether productivity, so measured, is the same thing as a life." — The writer's central claim is best summarized as —
choices: Notifications should be banned by federal regulation.
Smartphones have improved most workers' efficiency without significant cost.
The aggregate effect of small digital interruptions has reshaped working life in ways that have never been democratically debated.
Productivity metrics consistently overstate the contributions of skilled labor.
questions.correctText: The aggregate effect of small digital interruptions has reshaped working life in ways that have never been democratically debated.
questions.explanation: The passage hinges on the move from 'one at a time' (each notification reasonable) to 'considered together' (an unrecognized loss of agency), and ends by pointing out that workers were 'told' but not 'asked.' That is the argument.

questions.prompt: PASSAGE C: The juxtaposition of "Considered one at a time" with "Considered together" functions chiefly to —
choices: concede that the writer's argument applies only to a narrow group of workers.
shift the scale of analysis so that what seems trivial in isolation becomes consequential in aggregate.
introduce statistical evidence that the writer will defend in the next paragraph.
establish the writer's personal credentials as a workplace consultant.
questions.correctText: shift the scale of analysis so that what seems trivial in isolation becomes consequential in aggregate.
questions.explanation: This is a classic 'zoom-out' rhetorical move: the writer accepts a defense of the practice on micro grounds, then re-frames the same practice at macro scale to expose its costs. There is no concession of scope, no statistic, and no biography.

questions.prompt: PASSAGE C: The parallel construction "We have been told... We have not been asked..." most directly emphasizes —
choices: the writer's personal grievance against an unnamed employer.
the asymmetry between top-down assertion and bottom-up consent in the way the technology was adopted.
an unresolved grammatical ambiguity that the writer wants the reader to puzzle over.
a chronological sequence of historical events between the 1990s and today.
questions.correctText: the asymmetry between top-down assertion and bottom-up consent in the way the technology was adopted.
questions.explanation: Parallelism with a strategic swap (told / not asked) puts pressure on exactly the contrast the writer cares about — the difference between being informed of a change and being consulted about it.

questions.prompt: PASSAGE C: The phrase "a small, polite demand" works primarily through which strategy?
choices: Hyperbole — exaggerating the size of the demand to mock it.
Oxymoron — pairing courteous-sounding words with the underlying coercion they conceal.
Allusion — invoking a specific literary text the audience is expected to recognize.
Anecdote — recounting a single representative incident.
questions.correctText: Oxymoron — pairing courteous-sounding words with the underlying coercion they conceal.
questions.explanation: Calling a demand both 'small' and 'polite' yokes language of courtesy to language of compulsion; the tension exposes how interruptions disguise their coercive character. (Strictly, this is closer to a paradox or oxymoronic phrasing than to hyperbole, allusion, or anecdote.)

questions.prompt: PASSAGE C: The pronoun "we" throughout the passage primarily functions to —
choices: limit the argument's reach to a narrow occupational subgroup.
implicate the reader in the situation the writer is critiquing, making detachment harder.
signal that the writer is speaking on behalf of a specific named organization.
concede that the writer lacks personal experience with the phenomenon discussed.
questions.correctText: implicate the reader in the situation the writer is critiquing, making detachment harder.
questions.explanation: First-person plural collapses the distance between writer and reader, drafting the reader into the shared condition the essay names. It does the opposite of limiting scope or conceding distance.

questions.prompt: PASSAGE C: The writer's final sentence implies that —
choices: productivity and a meaningful life are necessarily opposed.
the prevailing definition of productivity may not capture what makes a life worth living, and that question deserves explicit consideration.
workers should refuse all forms of digital communication.
no measurable account of productivity is possible.
questions.correctText: the prevailing definition of productivity may not capture what makes a life worth living, and that question deserves explicit consideration.
questions.explanation: The qualifier 'so measured' is critical: the writer is not condemning productivity itself but the particular metric that has gone unquestioned. The claim is calibrated, not absolute.

questions.prompt: PASSAGE D: "What, to the American slave, is your 4th of July? I answer: a day that reveals to him, more than all other days in the year, the gross injustice and cruelty to which he is the constant victim." (Frederick Douglass, 1852) — The shift from "your" to "him" in this passage primarily serves to —
choices: indicate that the speaker no longer addresses the audience directly.
create a deliberate distance that exposes a contradiction between the audience's celebration and the slave's experience.
suggest the speaker is uncertain which group he means to address.
introduce a series of legal citations supporting his claim.
questions.correctText: create a deliberate distance that exposes a contradiction between the audience's celebration and the slave's experience.
questions.explanation: Douglass refuses to say 'our' Fourth of July. By keeping the holiday 'yours' and locating the slave in the third person, he forces his white audience to feel the moral gap between the freedom they celebrate and the bondage of those excluded from it.

questions.prompt: PASSAGE D: Douglass opens with a rhetorical question ("What, to the American slave, is your 4th of July?") rather than a direct assertion. This choice most likely serves to —
choices: invite his audience into the act of imagining the contradiction before he names it for them.
concede that the question has no satisfactory answer.
test whether his audience has read the speech in advance.
soften the speech to avoid offending listeners.
questions.correctText: invite his audience into the act of imagining the contradiction before he names it for them.
questions.explanation: A rhetorical question opens an interpretive space the speaker then fills. By posing the question first, Douglass invites the audience to think the unthinkable for a beat before he supplies his unsparing answer.

questions.prompt: PASSAGE D: The phrase "gross injustice and cruelty" relies primarily on which device?
choices: Understatement that ironically minimizes the harm described.
Pairing of two intensifying nouns to leave no room for euphemism or evasion.
Extended metaphor comparing slavery to a natural disaster.
Allusion to a specific Supreme Court ruling.
questions.correctText: Pairing of two intensifying nouns to leave no room for euphemism or evasion.
questions.explanation: Douglass deliberately stacks the harshest available nouns. The doublet ('injustice and cruelty') refuses the polite circumlocutions that allowed his audience to tolerate slavery; it is the opposite of understatement.

questions.prompt: PASSAGE D: Douglass's framing of the Fourth of July as a day that 'reveals' injustice 'more than all other days' relies primarily on which appeal?
choices: Logos through an explicit syllogism that lays out premises and conclusion.
Pathos generated by the irony that the audience's most cherished holiday becomes the sharpest accusation against them.
Ethos derived solely from the speaker's professional credentials.
Kairos through the citation of recent legislation.
questions.correctText: Pathos generated by the irony that the audience's most cherished holiday becomes the sharpest accusation against them.
questions.explanation: The rhetorical force comes from emotional irony: the celebration meant to mark American freedom turns into the day that most fully exposes American bondage. The argument runs through feeling, not formal syllogism.

questions.prompt: PASSAGE D: Douglass's primary purpose in this passage is to —
choices: thank his hosts for the invitation to speak on Independence Day.
indict his audience by refusing to let them enjoy a national celebration without confronting whom it excludes.
propose specific federal legislation to ameliorate working conditions.
recount the personal narrative of his own escape from slavery.
questions.correctText: indict his audience by refusing to let them enjoy a national celebration without confronting whom it excludes.
questions.explanation: The passage is a moral indictment delivered through the speaker's refusal to share in the holiday. Gratitude, legislation, and autobiography appear elsewhere in Douglass's work but are not the work of this excerpt.

questions.prompt: PASSAGE D: The implicit reasoning in the passage proceeds from —
choices: a specific statistical premise to a general legal conclusion.
a shared definition of liberty to the conclusion that any group excluded from that liberty stands as a living refutation of the nation's professed values.
an analogy between the Fourth of July and a religious holy day, which is then sustained throughout the speech.
an appeal to silence, suggesting that the topic cannot be discussed in public.
questions.correctText: a shared definition of liberty to the conclusion that any group excluded from that liberty stands as a living refutation of the nation's professed values.
questions.explanation: Douglass takes his audience's own definition of liberty (which the Fourth celebrates) and shows that the very existence of the American slave contradicts it. The argument runs by exposing internal contradiction.

questions.prompt: PASSAGE E: "Critics dismiss the neighborhood garden as a hobby for those who can afford the time. They miss the point. The garden produces tomatoes, yes; but it also produces the small, ordinary trust between neighbors who have learned each other's names while waiting for the rain. Of the two harvests, the second is harder to grow and easier to lose." — The writer's central claim is best summarized as —
choices: Community gardens are economically more efficient than commercial agriculture.
The most important yield of a community garden is the social trust it builds, which is fragile and undervalued.
Critics of community gardens are correct that the gardens are merely a leisure activity.
Cities should mandate the conversion of vacant lots into community gardens.
questions.correctText: The most important yield of a community garden is the social trust it builds, which is fragile and undervalued.
questions.explanation: The whole passage pivots on the metaphor of 'two harvests' — produce and trust — and ends by ranking the second above the first while marking its fragility. That is the thesis.

questions.prompt: PASSAGE E: "They miss the point." — This short, declarative sentence interrupts the longer surrounding sentences chiefly to —
choices: introduce a literal change in subject the writer will not return to.
deliver a blunt pivot that signals the writer is about to overturn the critics' framing.
concede that the critics are partially correct on the most important point.
demonstrate the writer's preference for fragments over complete sentences.
questions.correctText: deliver a blunt pivot that signals the writer is about to overturn the critics' framing.
questions.explanation: Short sentences placed amid longer ones do rhetorical work through contrast. Here, the curt declaration breaks the rhythm precisely at the moment the writer dismisses the critics' frame and pivots to a richer counter-argument.

questions.prompt: PASSAGE E: The phrase "the small, ordinary trust between neighbors" relies on which choice of diction to do most of its work?
choices: Elevated, abstract vocabulary that signals philosophical sophistication.
Modest, plainspoken adjectives that honor the everyday character of what the writer wants to defend.
Specialized technical vocabulary borrowed from sociology.
Archaic diction meant to evoke an idealized past.
questions.correctText: Modest, plainspoken adjectives that honor the everyday character of what the writer wants to defend.
questions.explanation: 'Small' and 'ordinary' deliberately understate the social good the writer is championing — a rhetorical move that protects the argument against the charge of sentimentality. Plain words for plain virtues.

questions.prompt: PASSAGE E: The closing sentence — "Of the two harvests, the second is harder to grow and easier to lose" — chiefly relies on which device?
choices: Antithesis built on parallel comparatives that sharpen the contrast between the two yields.
Allusion to a specific agricultural treatise the audience is expected to know.
Hyperbole exaggerating the difficulty of community formation.
Anecdote drawn from a single garden in a named city.
questions.correctText: Antithesis built on parallel comparatives that sharpen the contrast between the two yields.
questions.explanation: 'Harder to grow' is balanced against 'easier to lose' in tight parallel structure. The antithetical pair concentrates the argument's emotional weight into a single closing line.

questions.prompt: PASSAGE E: The writer most clearly addresses readers who —
choices: have already committed to expanding community gardens in their neighborhoods.
may be sympathetic to civic projects in principle but are vulnerable to the critics' efficiency-based dismissal.
specialize in urban agricultural policy at the federal level.
are openly hostile to any form of voluntary community organization.
questions.correctText: may be sympathetic to civic projects in principle but are vulnerable to the critics' efficiency-based dismissal.
questions.explanation: The passage names the critics' frame ('a hobby for those who can afford the time') in order to defuse it for readers who might have absorbed it without examination. The argument targets the persuadable middle, not specialists or opponents.

questions.prompt: PASSAGE E: Which assumption is most essential to the writer's argument?
choices: That commercial agriculture is incapable of producing nutritious food.
That informal social trust among neighbors is a genuine civic good rather than a sentimental notion.
That municipal governments are required to fund community gardens.
That the critics referenced in the first sentence have never visited a community garden.
questions.correctText: That informal social trust among neighbors is a genuine civic good rather than a sentimental notion.
questions.explanation: If a reader does not grant that social trust counts as a real good worth defending, the entire 'two harvests' argument collapses. The other choices are either irrelevant or unsupported by the passage.

questions.prompt: WRITING: Choose the revision that best corrects the underlined portion. "Neither the senator nor her aides _was prepared_ for the reporter's follow-up question."
choices: was prepared (no change)
were prepared
is prepared
has been prepared
questions.correctText: were prepared
questions.explanation: With 'neither...nor,' the verb agrees with the nearer subject. 'Her aides' is plural, so the verb must be 'were prepared.' This is one of the most-tested AP Lang grammar items.

questions.prompt: WRITING: Choose the revision that best corrects the underlined portion. "Every student should bring _their_ laptop to the workshop."
choices: their (no change — singular 'they' is now widely accepted in formal writing for an indefinite antecedent)
his
its
them
questions.correctText: their (no change — singular 'they' is now widely accepted in formal writing for an indefinite antecedent)
questions.explanation: The current AP Lang scoring and most major style guides (APA 7, MLA 9, Chicago 17) accept singular 'they' with indefinite antecedents like 'every student.' 'His' is gender-exclusive; 'its' is for non-persons; 'them' is the wrong case.

questions.prompt: WRITING: Choose the revision that best corrects the underlined portion. "The committee praised the candidate for her clear writing, her command of statistics, and _because she could explain complex ideas plainly_."
choices: because she could explain complex ideas plainly (no change)
her ability to explain complex ideas plainly
she explained complex ideas plainly
and explaining complex ideas plainly
questions.correctText: her ability to explain complex ideas plainly
questions.explanation: Items in a series must be grammatically parallel. The first two items are noun phrases ('her clear writing,' 'her command of statistics'), so the third must also be a noun phrase: 'her ability to explain...' The original mixes a subordinate clause into the series.

questions.prompt: WRITING: Choose the revision that best corrects the underlined sentence. "_Walking through the museum, the paintings seemed to come alive._"
choices: Walking through the museum, the paintings seemed to come alive. (no change)
Walking through the museum, we felt the paintings come alive.
The paintings, walking through the museum, seemed to come alive.
Walking through the museum was when the paintings came alive.
questions.correctText: Walking through the museum, we felt the paintings come alive.
questions.explanation: The original is a dangling modifier — paintings don't walk. The revision supplies a logical agent ('we') for the introductory participial phrase. Option C makes the dangler worse; D buries the action in a vague 'was when' construction.

questions.prompt: WRITING: Choose the most concise and precise revision. "_Due to the fact that_ the bridge was closed, traffic was rerouted along the river road."
choices: Due to the fact that (no change)
Because
Owing to the fact that
On account of the fact that
questions.correctText: Because
questions.explanation: AP Lang readers consistently reward concision when meaning is preserved. 'Because' replaces a four-word filler phrase with a single word that says exactly the same thing. The other options are merely synonyms for the wordy original.

questions.prompt: WRITING: A formal research essay on climate policy contains the sentence: "Scientists have argued for decades that the trends are clear; _basically, anyone who doesn't get this is asleep at the wheel_." Which revision best preserves the writer's argument while restoring a tone consistent with the rest of the essay?
choices: basically, anyone who doesn't get this is asleep at the wheel (no change)
the persistence of skepticism therefore reflects a failure of attention rather than a deficiency of evidence.
and people who don't believe it are basically clueless about the science.
skeptics need to wake up and smell the coffee already.
questions.correctText: the persistence of skepticism therefore reflects a failure of attention rather than a deficiency of evidence.
questions.explanation: Option B keeps the writer's argument (skepticism stems from inattention, not from missing evidence) but restores academic register. The other choices preserve or worsen the colloquial slang that clashes with a formal essay.

questions.prompt: WRITING: Choose the transition that best fits between these two sentences in an argumentative essay: "The new policy reduced emissions by twelve percent in the first year. _____, energy costs for low-income households rose sharply, prompting calls for targeted rebates."
choices: Consequently
However
Similarly
For example
questions.correctText: However
questions.explanation: The second sentence introduces a counterweight to the first (a benefit followed by a cost), so a contrastive transition is required. 'Consequently' would suggest the cost was caused by the emissions reduction (not stated); 'Similarly' and 'For example' would misrepresent the relationship as continuation rather than contrast.

questions.prompt: WRITING: Which sentence uses the semicolon correctly?
choices: The mayor announced three priorities; housing, transit, and parks.
Although the report was delayed; the committee voted on schedule.
The witness testified for two hours; the jury then asked for a recess.
The author cited three sources; including a recent monograph.
questions.correctText: The witness testified for two hours; the jury then asked for a recess.
questions.explanation: A semicolon joins two independent clauses that could each stand alone as sentences. Option A should use a colon (list following an independent clause). B should use a comma after the subordinate clause. D should also use a comma before 'including.'

questions.prompt: WRITING: Choose the sentence with correct comma usage.
choices: The novelist, who won the prize last spring will speak on Friday.
The novelist who won the prize last spring, will speak on Friday.
The novelist, who won the prize last spring, will speak on Friday.
The novelist who, won the prize last spring will speak on Friday.
questions.correctText: The novelist, who won the prize last spring, will speak on Friday.
questions.explanation: A non-restrictive relative clause ('who won the prize last spring') — one that supplies extra information rather than identifying which novelist — is set off by a pair of commas. The clause must be enclosed on both sides; partial commas (A, B) and a misplaced comma (D) are all incorrect.

questions.prompt: WRITING: Choose the sentence with correct apostrophe use.
choices: The students assignments were due on Tuesday, but its grading was delayed until Friday.
The students' assignments were due on Tuesday, but its grading was delayed until Friday.
The student's assignments were due on Tuesday, but it's grading was delayed until Friday.
The students' assignments were due on Tuesday, but it's grading was delayed until Friday.
questions.correctText: The students' assignments were due on Tuesday, but its grading was delayed until Friday.
questions.explanation: Plural possessive 'students'' takes an apostrophe after the s; possessive 'its' takes no apostrophe ('it's' = 'it is'). Only option B gets both right.

questions.prompt: WRITING: A paragraph reads: "The lab opens at eight. The director arrives at nine. The technicians begin at nine-thirty. The first experiments start at ten." Which revision best improves sentence variety while preserving meaning?
choices: Leave as is — the parallel short sentences create useful rhythm.
The lab opens at eight; the director arrives an hour later, and by nine-thirty the technicians have begun, allowing the first experiments to start at ten.
At eight, the lab opens, and at nine, the director arrives, and at nine-thirty, the technicians begin, and at ten, the experiments start.
Eight: lab opens. Nine: director arrives. Nine-thirty: technicians begin. Ten: experiments start.
questions.correctText: The lab opens at eight; the director arrives an hour later, and by nine-thirty the technicians have begun, allowing the first experiments to start at ten.
questions.explanation: Option B varies clause length and connector type and uses subordination to mark the relationships among events. Option A and D both keep the choppy original; C strings everything together with repetitive 'and...and...and' coordination.

questions.prompt: WRITING: A campaign brochure includes the sentence: "_Mistakes were made by the previous administration._" When the writer's goal is to assign clear responsibility, the revision that best serves that goal is —
choices: Mistakes were made by the previous administration. (no change — passive is more diplomatic)
The previous administration made mistakes.
Mistakes were made.
There were mistakes that occurred during the previous administration.
questions.correctText: The previous administration made mistakes.
questions.explanation: Active voice names the agent first and clearly. The original passive construction technically names the agent but de-emphasizes responsibility by burying it in a 'by' phrase. C drops the agent entirely; D buries the action in expletive ('There were') and vague ('occurred') wording. When responsibility is the rhetorical goal, B is strongest.

questions.prompt: WRITING: A student's introductory paragraph contains the following four sentences in this order: (1) Several cities have piloted free bus service. (2) Public transit has become a major equity issue in American cities. (3) Their results offer important lessons for federal policy. (4) Riders without cars depend on it; commuters with cars benefit when others use it. — Which reordering produces the most logical paragraph?
questions.explanation: Move from general to specific: name the issue (2), explain why it matters to a broad audience (4), introduce the concrete cases (1), then point to their significance (3). This pattern — claim, stakes, evidence, implication — is the standard structure for an analytical introduction.

questions.prompt: WRITING: Which of the following is the most effective thesis statement for a rhetorical analysis essay on a presidential inaugural address?
choices: The speech was very inspiring and used many rhetorical devices.

In her inaugural address, the new president unifies a divided audience by pairing inclusive first-person plural with carefully chosen historical allusions that recast partisan disagreement as a shared American tradition.
This essay will discuss the rhetorical strategies in the inaugural address.
The president gave a good speech that touched on many important topics, such as the economy, foreign policy, and unity.
questions.correctText: In her inaugural address, the new president unifies a divided audience by pairing inclusive first-person plural with carefully chosen historical allusions that recast partisan disagreement as a shared American tradition.
questions.explanation: A strong rhetorical-analysis thesis names (a) the rhetorical goal, (b) the specific strategies used to achieve it, and (c) the effect on the audience. Option B does all three. A is evaluative but vague; C is a roadmap, not a claim; D is a topic list.

questions.prompt: WRITING: A paragraph defends the value of close reading. Which sentence, if inserted as the topic sentence at the start, would best unify the paragraph below? "_____. A student who pauses on a single metaphor learns to ask why the writer chose that image and not another. The habit transfers: in a contract, in a news article, in a campaign speech, the same trained attention catches what would otherwise slip past unnoticed."
choices: Close reading is a difficult skill that most students do not master.
Close reading trains a kind of attention that pays dividends well beyond the literature classroom.
Many teachers assign too much reading and not enough close reading.
Reading is one of the most important skills a student can learn.
questions.correctText: Close reading trains a kind of attention that pays dividends well beyond the literature classroom.
questions.explanation: The body of the paragraph illustrates how the attention developed in literature class transfers to contracts, news, and speeches — exactly the claim made in option B. A is off-topic (difficulty), C is off-topic (curriculum design), and D is too general to organize the specifics that follow.
